_notes: Application-oriented taxonomy: categories describe what AI systems DO, not abstract policy domains
match_tags: entries with ANY of these tags (case-insensitive partial match) are pulled into the topic
match_fields: entries with ANY of these fields are also considered relevant
reading_paths: curated sequences per audience — each entry references an id from any content JSON
audience keys: student | general | policy | legal | economist | technologist

topics.name: Product Liability & AI
topics.description: When AI systems cause harm, who is responsible? This canon covers tort doctrine, causation challenges, liability allocation across the AI supply chain, and the frameworks courts and regulators are developing to assign accountability for algorithmic harm.

student.label: Student
student.description: Understand how traditional liability doctrine struggles with algorithmic systems.

sequence.note: Start here — why algorithmic systems fracture the causal chain tort law requires
Industry perspective on state AI liability proposals
The unfairness standard as an alternative to traditional tort

general.label: General Reader
general.description: Who pays when AI goes wrong — and why it's complicated.

sequence.note: Why proving AI caused your harm is harder than it sounds
Who bears the cost when companies deploy first and ask questions later

policy.description: Liability frameworks, safe harbors, and the state legislative landscape.

sequence.note: Industry testimony on Illinois AI liability bills — safe harbor debates

UDAAP unfairness as a regulatory alternative to tort

legal.label: Legal Professional
legal.description: Doctrinal analysis of causation, duty, and emerging liability frameworks.

economist.label: Economist
economist.description: Economic analysis of liability regimes and harm allocation.

sequence.note: Consumer harm independent of collusion — pricing regulation as alternative

technologist.label: Technologist
technologist.description: Technical dimensions of traceability, auditability, and liability.

sequence.note: Scaling laws for algorithmic auditing under real constraints

topics.name: Moderation, Detection, & Enforcement
topics.description: How platforms identify, filter, and remove content — and the legal frameworks governing these decisions. This canon covers Section 230, content moderation systems, automated detection, and the First Amendment dimensions of platform speech.

topics.name: Identification, Authentication, & Biometrics
topics.description: AI systems that identify, verify, and track individuals — from facial recognition to behavioral biometrics to identity verification. This canon covers the privacy, civil liberties, and data governance dimensions of identification technologies.

topics.name: Generative & Synthetic Media
topics.description: AI systems that create text, images, audio, and video — and the intellectual property, authenticity, and trust questions they raise. This canon covers copyright and training data, deepfakes, synthetic content provenance, and the liability regimes for generative outputs.

topics.name: Cyber-Physical & Embodied AI
topics.description: AI systems that interact with the physical world — autonomous vehicles, robotics, drones, and infrastructure. This canon covers safety standards, physical harm liability, and the governance challenges unique to systems that can cause tangible damage.
